text: The surgical team begins with an endoscopy, revealing narrowing of the third portion of the duodenum between the aorta and the SMA, with visible vessel pulsations. Laparoscopically, the transverse colon is reflected cranially to identify the middle colic vessels and the SMA's takeoff. The SMA is seen crossing the third portion of the duodenum on the screen's left. Endoscopy confirms the narrowing at the SMA crossing. The duodenum is mobilized, and the transverse mesocolon is incised to expose it. The lateral portion of the second part of the duodenum is opened, and a limb of jejunum is brought up for a lateral side-to-side anastomosis.

The surgeon places a stay suture between the jejunum and duodenum to orient the anastomosis. An enterotomy is made in both the duodenum and jejunum, allowing the passage of a linear stapler. A white load, 45mm stapler is carefully positioned and fired, avoiding compromise of the transverse mesocolon or the SMA at the distal tip. The staple line is inspected, confirmed to be hemostatic and widely patent. The defect is then over-sewn in a hand-sewn two-layer fashion to prevent narrowing of the anastomosis.

The surgeon places a stay suture inferiorly to orient the suture line, followed by the closure of the common defect using two layers of Vicryl suture. The second layer is completed with a lapar tie. The anastomosis is inspected, showing a lateral side-to-side anastomosis. An endoscopy is performed, confirming the anastomosis is wide and hemostatic. The third portion of the duodenum is visible distally, narrowed between pulsatile vessels, with the anastomosis several centimeters proximal on the right.
